steps.title: Repeated trials, predictable shape
steps.body: Binomial models count successes across a fixed number of independent yes/no trials with constant probability. You'll identify valid binomial settings, compute probabilities, and interpret the mean, standard deviation, and shape of the distribution.

definitions.definition: A fixed number n of independent trials, each a success/failure with the same probability p.
The number of ways to choose k successes out of n trials, ignoring order.
P(k successes) = C(n, k) × p^k × (1 − p)^(n − k).
The expected number of successes: μ = np.
Spread of the success count: σ = √(np(1 − p)).
Symmetric when p ≈ 0.5; skewed right for small p and left for large p.

steps.title: Identify a binomial setting
steps.context: Pick the scenario that fits all binomial conditions.
steps.prompt: Which chart shows a binomial pattern (fixed trials, two outcomes)?
hints: Check: fixed n, two outcomes, independent trials, constant p.
Without replacement changes p each draw, breaking a key binomial condition.

feedback.wrong: A binomial process needs fixed n, independent yes/no trials, and constant p.

solution.body: Twenty fair-coin flips are binomial: fixed n = 20, success/failure outcomes, independent flips, and constant p = 0.5.
solution.takeaway: Always test all four binomial assumptions before calculating.

steps.title: Compute a combination
steps.context: You need the coefficient in a binomial probability.

solution.takeaway: Combinations count selections when order does not matter.

steps.title: Exactly k successes
steps.context: A 6-question multiple-choice quiz has one correct option out of four per question. A student guesses on every question.
steps.prompt: Find P(exactly 2 correct), where X ~ Bin(6, 0.25).

feedback.wrong: Apply the binomial PMF with n = 6, k = 2, p = 0.25.

solution.takeaway: The PMF combines counting (nCk) with weighted success/failure powers.

steps.title: At least one failure
steps.context: Each of 4 components passes inspection with probability 0.9, independently.
steps.prompt: Find P(at least one component fails).
hints: Use the complement: at least one fail = 1 - none fail.
None fail means all four pass: 0.9^4.

solution.takeaway: Complement methods simplify many "at least one" binomial questions.

steps.title: Binomial mean
steps.prompt: What is the mean of X?
hints: For a binomial variable, mean mu = np.

solution.takeaway: Binomial mean gives the long-run average count of successes.

steps.title: Binomial standard deviation
steps.prompt: Approximate the standard deviation of Y.

feedback.wrong: Use sigma = sqrt(np(1-p)) and round appropriately.

solution.takeaway: Binomial spread depends on n and both p and 1-p.

steps.title: Binomial distribution explorer
steps.prompt: Slide n and p, watch bars move, and track how mean, SD, and skewness change in real time.

feedback.ready: Binomial center is np, spread is sqrt(np(1-p)), and shape depends mostly on p.

steps.title: Read center and skew
steps.prompt: Which binomial shape has center near 3 and a right tail (p = 0.25, n = 12)?
hints: When p < 0.5, the binomial shape is usually skewed right.

feedback.wrong: Use mu = np for center and compare p to 0.5 for skew direction.

solution.body: mu = 12(0.25) = 3, and because p < 0.5 the mass piles near small counts with a right tail.
solution.takeaway: Center comes from np; skew comes from how far p is from 0.5.

steps.title: Quality check setup
steps.context: A factory tests 8 light bulbs. Each bulb works with probability 0.7, independently.
steps.prompt: Is X = number of working bulbs binomial?

options.label: No, because n must be larger than 30

hints: Check the four binomial conditions one by one.
Here n is fixed, outcomes are success/failure, trials are independent, and p stays 0.7.

feedback.wrong: All binomial assumptions are satisfied in this scenario.

solution.body: Yes. This is a binomial count with n = 8 and p = 0.7.
solution.takeaway: Binomial modeling starts with assumptions, not formulas.

feedback.wrong: Use the binomial PMF with n = 8, k = 6, p = 0.7.

solution.takeaway: Exact-k probabilities combine one coefficient with two powers.

steps.title: At least one failure
steps.prompt: Approximate P(at least one bulb fails).
hints: Use the complement of all bulbs working.

solution.takeaway: Capstone workflow: validate binomial assumptions, compute exact-k, then use complements for at-least-one.

steps.title: Chapter 4 complete
steps.body:
You classified binomial experiments, computed coefficients and PMF values, and interpreted center, spread, and skew.

Next up: normal curves, z-scores, areas, and sampling distributions.
